title: Checkpoint
html:
We reach the clearing in under a minute, the undergrowth thin enough to move through without breaking stride.

It's roughly 20 meters across with a stone pillar at the center, about 2 meters tall, flat-topped, its surface carved with markings that give off a faint blue glow. Fifteen or so people from the wider group have already arrived from other directions and are standing around it in a loose ring at maybe 3 meters' radius, nobody touching it, watching it the way you'd watch something that hasn't decided what it is yet.

Soo-Yun walks past me and puts her hand flat on the stone.

The markings pulse once, bright enough that the nearest people step back, and the glow settles to something steady. A notification appears in my vision.

---

Checkpoint Reached.

Class Assignment: Active.

Approach the Waystone to receive your class.

---

"It's fine," Soo-Yun says, pulling her hand back and checking her palm. Nothing burned. "We all need to touch it."

The gym teacher looks at me. I walk up and press my hand to the stone. It's cold and smooth, and the glow from the carvings is warm enough to cast light between my fingers. The notification changes.

---

Analyzing Participant...

Class Assigned: [Scavenger]

A class suited to resourcefulness and improvisation.

Primary stat growth: Agility. Secondary: Perception.

Scavenger Passive: Environmental Advantage — gain a 15% bonus to actions taken using found or improvised tools.

---

I pull my hand back while the stat block is still visible.

---

[Alias: Eric]

Level: 2

Class: Scavenger

HP: 110 / 110

Agility: 14

Perception: 12

Strength: 9

Endurance: 10

Available Skill Points: 2

---

The block fades after a few seconds. Scavenger. I killed a kappa with a dead branch and the system classified me as someone who improvises with whatever's on the ground. The 15% bonus to improvised tools is useful in a world where I own exactly one machete I picked up off the forest floor. I'm not going to argue with it.

The gym teacher goes next, pressing both hands to the stone out of some reflex, like he's bracing against a wall for a push-up he didn't volunteer for.

---

Class Assigned: [Guardian]

A class built for physical endurance and protection.

Primary stat growth: Strength. Secondary: Endurance.

Guardian Passive: Steadfast — reduce incoming physical damage by 10% when stationary.

---

"Guardian," he says when it fades, lips still moving from the read.

"Does it fit?" I ask.

"I teach gym," he mutters, more to the stone than to me.

Fair enough.

The woman from the restaurant goes last from our group. She walks up without being asked, puts one finger to the surface, and reads whatever appears in her own vision. When it fades she stands still for a moment longer than the others did, looking at nothing, then steps back and goes straight back to watching the far tree line. She doesn't share the class name. Nobody asks.

Soo-Yun reads hers out in a low voice. "Ranger. Perception primary, Agility secondary. Ranger Active: Thrown Accuracy — increase hit rate for thrown projectiles by 20%."

The gym teacher looks at her.

"She threw a rock at a kappa," I tell him.

"Before or after the system assigned her anything?"

"Before."

He looks at the pillar, then back at her, like he's recalculating something he thought he understood about how this system works.

About 40 people have filtered into the clearing by now, touching the pillar in small groups, reading their notifications in near-silence. The protest noise from the glass room is completely gone. People cluster and talk in low voices and mostly keep their eyes moving between the tree line and each other. The heavyset man from the forest's edge is here too. He touches the pillar, reads, and something in his expression shifts in a way he shuts down fast. He doesn't share the result.

---

Tutorial Update:

Checkpoint 1 — Complete.

Next Objective: Reach the Tutorial Exit.

Time Remaining: 1 hour 42 minutes.

Caution: Multiple hostile entities detected between your position and the exit.

Proceed with care.

---

"1 hour 42," I say.

"That's not a lot," Soo-Yun says.

The gym teacher shifts his weight. "Which direction is the exit?"

"System didn't say." I look at the tree line across the clearing from where we came in. The light there is ordinary forest shadow. "Standing here doesn't close the gap."

The woman from the restaurant has been facing that far tree line for the last 2 minutes, watching it the way she watched the kappa fight. She hasn't moved toward it, but she's been ready to for a while.

I still don't know her name. I don't know the gym teacher's either. Close to an hour with these people, a dead kappa between us, and I haven't asked once. That's either efficiency or something I'll look at later.

"What's your name?" I ask the gym teacher.

"Park Jae-won," he says.

"And you?" I look at the woman.

She takes a second. "Han Mi-rae."

"More than I expected. She's given me one-syllable reactions since the glass room and this is the most she's said to me at once.

"I'm Eric," I tell them.

Soo-Yun opens her mouth.

"Don't," I say.

She closes it. Jae-won looks between us.

"Not worth explaining," I tell him.

The clearing is thinning out. Some people head for the far tree line, some move south along the edge, a few drift back toward the glass room, which makes no sense but people under pressure make bad geometry. The heavyset man joins a group of 6 heading south, moving faster than he looked like he could, and doesn't look back. It's the same direction I'm thinking, which either means he's read something useful or he's following someone who has.

The system said multiple hostiles between here and the exit without saying where the exit is. That either means every direction has something in it, or the warning covers the whole zone. It doesn't change the basic problem: we're not getting to the exit clean.

"There's movement in there," Mi-rae says.

She's still watching the far tree line. I look where she's looking. She's right. Something in the undergrowth, maybe 10 meters back from the clearing edge, moving parallel to us rather than toward us. It's keeping pace. Another shape picks up beside it, slightly further back, same direction.

They're not charging. They're running the tree line the same way the first kappa stood at the edge of the glass room and watched the group before it picked a target. Patient. Whatever the tutorial put in this forest, it gave it patience.

'Smarter than I thought.'

"Two of them," Mi-rae says. "Moving together."

2 kappas running parallel to the clearing while the timer ticks. The Steadfast passive on Jae-won's class cuts incoming damage when he's stationary, which is useful in exactly the kind of situation I don't want to be in: standing still while something that already knows how to use a machete closes on us.

"We're not going that way," I say.

"Where then?" Jae-won asks.

I scan the clearing edge. The group that went south is already at the tree line, 15 or so people. The kappas are tracking the north side of the far tree line, running parallel. The sides of the clearing are what they're not watching.

"South. Behind the bigger group." I start moving. "More targets means it splits the attention."

"They don't know we're doing that," Jae-won says, keeping up.

"I know."

"Rocks," I tell Soo-Yun.

She's already picking them up, 2 of them, adding them to her jacket pocket alongside the 2 she's still carrying from the clearing's edge. 4 rocks total, and the kappas we've seen have needed 1 good hit each, which means we have margin if she doesn't miss. That's the whole plan right now.

Mi-rae falls in without being asked. Jae-won brings up the rear, still glancing over his shoulder at the far tree line every few steps. I don't look back. The kappas either follow us or they take one of the other groups, and knowing which isn't going to change the direction I'm already walking.

The southern tree line is 25 meters out. The group ahead of us is already absorbed into the undergrowth, their voices fading. The machete grip has gone warm from my hand, which is a small and useless thing to notice, but I notice it.

The exit is somewhere in there. The clock says 1 hour 39 minutes and the system has already confirmed it won't be a clean path. 4 people with a machete and some rocks, moving behind a group that doesn't know we're using them as a buffer, heading toward something the tutorial is calling an exit without telling us what that actually looks like. The only variable I can actually control right now is how prepared we are when we hit the next thing.

I tighten my grip and cross into the tree line.

The undergrowth here is denser than it was on the north side. The canopy closes overhead and the light drops, not dark but filtered, green-grey and flat. The group ahead of us is audible but not visible: feet on soft ground, low voices, the occasional branch pushed aside. We're maybe 20 meters behind them.

Jae-won is breathing harder than the pace warrants. I don't comment. Mi-rae moves quietly for someone running on fumes, her eyes sweeping left and right in a steady pattern rather than fixing on any single point. That's a better habit than I expected.

The forest goes flat for about 50 meters and then the ground slopes downward, gradual at first, then enough to feel in the knees. The group ahead slows. I slow with them.

At the base of the slope the trees open around a narrow creek, maybe 2 meters across, the water dark and moving slowly over flat stones. The group ahead stops at the bank. A few step in without looking and pull back fast when the water reaches their shins.

‘Kappas,’ Soo-Yun says quietly, right beside my ear.

On the far bank, half in the water and half on the stones, 2 kappas are crouched over something I can't make out from this distance. They haven't looked up. The group at the bank hasn't seen them yet.

The kappas' head-bowls are full. Whatever Soo-Yun did in the clearing was a stationary target at close range. Across 2 meters of moving water in low light, with the targets crouched, is a different number.

"Can you hit them from here?" I ask, keeping my voice below the creek noise.

"Maybe one," she says, turning a rock in her hand to feel the weight. "If the first one doesn't alert the second before I can reload."

"How fast can you reload?"

She looks at the creek, then the kappas. "Not that fast."

The group at the bank is starting to work out what they're looking at. A woman near the front goes completely still and grabs the arm of the person beside her. No screaming yet, which is the most useful thing they've done since the glass room.

Mi-rae crouches beside me. She picks up a stone from the ground, checks it, sets it down, finds another. She doesn't say anything.

2 kappas with full head-bowls, a 2-meter crossing, 30-odd people who don't know what spills the water. The moment the bank group panics, anyone who goes into that creek while the kappas are still standing is going to draw the machete.

"We cross first," I say.

Jae-won looks at me. "The four of us."

"Fast across, hit before they orient, use the creek water if you can get them into it. Soo-Yun: first rock goes to the near one the second my feet hit the far bank. Mi-rae: second target." I check my grip on the machete. "Move."

The creek hits my ankles and then my knees and the cold drives straight up through my legs. The crossing makes a Splash! that sounds twice as loud as it should in the quiet, and both kappas look up at once.
